purpose: Generalization test: do the rules tuned on 'countries' (batches 1-3) transfer to a totally different domain? Elements = science register, answers are substances not places, dimension-rich, listable only via the famous elements (stress-tests the listable-set rule). Same playbook: reflex-defeaters, varied dimension KINDS, named metrics, no figure-hints. NOT filed to the library.
watch_for: Obscurity creeping in where countries couldn't (e.g. caesium, rhodium less famous than the finalists) — that's the elements-specific failure mode to judge.

candidates.q: Which element makes up more of the Earth's rocky crust than any other?
candidates.note: Defeats the iron/silicon reflex — most picture a metal. Finalists Oxygen, Silicon, Aluminum, Iron.

candidates.q: Which metal is a liquid at ordinary room temperature?
candidates.note: Gallium and caesium melt only when warmed past room temp. Finalists Mercury, Gallium, Caesium.

candidates.q: Which metal must be heated hotter than any other before it melts?
candidates.note: Metric pinned to 'metal' on purpose: carbon arguably melts higher, so the unqualified version is contested. Finalists Tungsten, Tantalum, Rhenium, Osmium.

candidates.q: Which metal weighs the least for its size — the least dense of them all?
candidates.note: So light it floats on water (so do sodium/potassium, hence 'least dense' not 'floats'). Finalists Lithium, Sodium, Potassium.

candidates.q: Which metal carries electricity better than any other?
candidates.note: Edges copper — defeats the copper reflex. Finalists Silver, Copper, Gold, Aluminum.

candidates.q: Which metal is usually the most expensive of all the precious metals?
candidates.note: Beats gold and platinum; ~100x rarer than gold. Flag: price is volatile (hit ~$29k in 2021). No named comparator in the stem. Finalists Rhodium, Gold, Platinum, Palladium.

candidates.q: Which element makes up most of the air we breathe?
candidates.note: Defeats the oxygen reflex. Finalists Nitrogen, Oxygen, Argon, Carbon dioxide(not an element).

candidates.q: What is the heaviest element that is not radioactive — the last truly stable one?
candidates.note: Flag: bismuth (one heavier) was thought stable but is faintly radioactive (half-life ~10^19 yr), so lead is the textbook answer. Finalists Lead, Bismuth, Uranium.

candidates.q: Which metal is so reactive it bursts into flame the instant it touches water — the most reactive of them all?
candidates.note: Francium is technically more reactive but too rare/unstable to be a real candidate; flag it. Defeats the sodium reflex. Finalists Caesium, Rubidium, Potassium, Sodium.

candidates.q: Which gas, lighter than air and unable to catch fire, is used to fill floating balloons?
candidates.note: Defeats the hydrogen reflex (hydrogen is lighter but flammable — the 'won't burn' clause is the test). Finalists Helium, Hydrogen, Neon.

candidates.q: Which element, in one of its forms, is the hardest natural material on Earth?
Which metal is used to make the strongest permanent magnets?
candidates.note: Defeats the iron reflex; neodymium-iron-boron magnets are the strongest. Finalists Neodymium, Iron, Cobalt, Samarium.
